query:
You are a venture-capital analyst. For each of the 10 companies below, build an identity + financial profile from Crunchbase, Pitchbook, Dealroom, TechCrunch, Les Echos, official press releases, and LinkedIn About sections.

Companies:
  1. name="Coval", website="https://coval.ai"
  2. name="SiliconFlow", website="https://siliconflow.com"
  3. name="Together AI", website="https://together.ai"
  4. name="Workato", website="https://orkato.com"
  5. name="Northflank", website="https://northflank.com"
  6. name="Botpress", website="https://botpress.com"
  7. name="Hugging Face", website="https://huggingface.co"
  8. name="Altair AI Studio", website="https://altair.com"
  9. name="Fermyon", website="https://fermyon.com"
  10. name="AWS Bedrock", website="https://aws.amazon.com"

For EACH company extract:
- name (EXACTLY as listed above), website, hq_city, hq_country, founded_year
- employee_count (current LinkedIn headcount as integer)
- employee_growth_yoy (year-over-year change as decimal: +12% → 0.12)
- funding_stage (Seed / Series A / B / C / Public / Bootstrapped)
- funding_status: 'Funded' if any disclosed round; 'Bootstrapped' if founders publicly state self-funded / no VC / never raised; 'Stealth' if company explicitly operating in stealth mode; 'Unknown' otherwise.
- funding_total_usd (cumulative raised in USD)
- last_round_amount_usd, last_round_date (YYYY-MM-DD), last_round_type
- funding_rounds: COMPLETE history — array of {round, amount_usd, date, lead} ordered chronologically. Aim for 2-5 rounds when available.
- notable_investors: distinct VCs/angels/corporates across all rounds. For each: {name, domain}. The domain is the investor's website (e.g. 'sequoiacap.com' for Sequoia Capital) — critical for logo display.
- arr_usd: ONLY if publicly disclosed (funding press, podcast). Do NOT estimate.
- customer_count: ONLY if officially announced.
- avg_contract_usd: ONLY if disclosed.
- acquisition: {acquired, acquirer, amount_eur, year, source_url}. Return acquired=false if not acquired.

Omit any field you cannot verify — do NOT pad with placeholders or guess. Return one object per company in the SAME order as the input list.
answer:
{"executive_summary": "- **Seed-Stage Early Movers**: Coval (2024, $3.8M raised) and SiliconFlow (2023, ~$49M raised) represent the newest entrants, both still at single-digit employee counts, signaling high execution risk but early-mover advantage in AI agent testing and inference infrastructure respectively.
- **Series B Mega-Rounds Dominate AI Inference**: Together AI's $305M Series B (Feb 2025) at a $3.3B valuation and Hugging Face's $235M Series D (Aug 2023) at $4.5B show that open-source AI infrastructure attracts outsized rounds; investors should expect continued capital concentration.
- **Late-Stage iPaaS Monetization**: Workato's $150M ARR and 11K customers at a $5.7B valuation demonstrate that integration platforms can scale efficiently; the $13.6K avg ACV suggests land-and-expand is working.
- **Product-Not-Company Entries**: Altair AI Studio and AWS Bedrock are products of public entities (Altair/Siemens and Amazon), not venture-backed startups; analysts must avoid treating them as comparable venture opportunities.
- **Acquisition Signal from WebAssembly**: Fermyon's acquisition by Akamai (Dec 2025) after raising only $26M validates that serverless WebAssembly is a strategic asset for edge-compute players; acquisition price was undisclosed.
- **Cross-Border China Funding**: SiliconFlow's nine-figure RMB Series A led by Alibaba Cloud illustrates how Chinese AI infrastructure companies can raise substantial capital domestically while targeting global markets from a Singapore legal entity.
- **Canadian AI Agent Momentum**: Botpress's $25M Series B (Jun 2025) led by FRAMEWORK from Quebec City shows AI agent platforms can attract top-tier capital outside traditional US hubs.

It became generally available on September 28, 2023 [10_aws_bedrock[0]] [94]. The official Bedrock page states it "powers generative AI for more than 100,000 organizations worldwide" [10_aws_bedrock[1]] [47]. As an internal AWS service, Bedrock has no separate venture funding rounds, investor list, ARR, or employee headcount. The April 2023 preview and September 2023 GA are the relevant launch dates [10_aws_bedrock[2]] [96].

---", "comparative_analysis": "| Company | Stage | Total Raised (USD) | Employees | Founded | HQ Country | Acquired? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coval | Seed | $3.8M | 10 | 2024 | US | No |
| SiliconFlow | Series A | ~$49M | 15 | 2023 | CN | No |
| Together AI | Series B | $533.5M | 287 | 2022 | US | No |
| Workato | Series E | $415M | 1,063 | 2013 | US | No |
| Northflank | Series A | $24.9M | 25 | 2019 | UK | No |
| Botpress | Series B | $40M | 127 | 2017 | CA | No |
| Hugging Face | Series D | $400M | 679 | 2016 | US | No |
| Altair AI Studio | Public | N/A (product) | 4,641 | 1985 | US | Parent acquired |
| Fermyon | Series A | $26M | 11-50 | 2021 | US | Yes (Akamai) |
| AWS Bedrock | Public | N/A (service) | N/A | 2023 | US | No |

The capital distribution is highly skewed: Together AI, Workato, and Hugging Face account for over $1.3B of the approximately $1.46B in total venture capital raised across the seven truly venture-backed entities. The remaining four venture-backed companies (Coval, SiliconFlow, Northflank, Botpress) have raised a combined ~$117M, underscoring the winner-take-most dynamics in AI infrastructure. Two entries (Altair AI Studio, AWS Bedrock) are products of public entities and should not be evaluated as venture opportunities.

---", "synthesis": "**Capital Concentration vs. Stage Diversification.** The seven venture-backed companies span Seed (Coval) through Series E (Workato), but capital is massively concentrated at the late stage: Together AI alone raised $533.5M, more than the bottom five venture-backed companies combined. This reflects the capital intensity of AI compute infrastructure, where GPU procurement and model serving require enormous upfront investment. The mechanism is straightforward: inference and training infrastructure has high fixed costs (GPU clusters) and strong economies of scale, meaning only well-capitalized players can offer competitive pricing. The implication for investors is that early-stage bets in this space (Coval, Northflank) carry not just technology risk but also capital risk - they may need to raise very large rounds to compete, or find defensible niches (agent testing, developer platforms) that don't require GPU ownership.

**Product-vs-Company Misclassification Risk.** Two of the ten entries - Altair AI Studio and AWS Bedrock - are products within larger public entities, not standalone companies. Treating them as venture opportunities would produce misleading comparisons. Altair AI Studio is a renamed RapidMiner Studio within Altair Engineering (now a Siemens subsidiary); AWS Bedrock is an AWS service. Neither has independent financials, funding rounds, or investor syndicates. The divergence between these "companies" and the true startups reveals a classification error that VC analysts must actively filter: when scanning competitive landscapes, product names can masquerade as company names, inflating apparent market fragmentation.

**Acquisition as Validation - and Limitation.** Fermyon's acquisition by Akamai after raising only $26M validates the strategic value of serverless WebAssembly for edge computing, but the undisclosed price means investors cannot benchmark the return. Pitchbook confirms the acquisition date (November 25, 2025) but no amount was disclosed in the Akamai press release. This is a common pattern in smaller strategic acquisitions: the acquirer perceives technology/team value but the deal size doesn't warrant disclosure. For VC portfolio construction, this means Fermyon-style outcomes may be more frequent than large exits in the WebAssembly niche, but they are harder to model.

**Cross-Border Funding Dynamics.** SiliconFlow's structure - legally registered in Singapore, operating in Beijing, raising RMB-denominated rounds from Chinese investors (Alibaba Cloud, Sinovation Ventures) - illustrates the growing bifurcation in AI infrastructure funding. Western VC databases (CB Insights, Crunchbase) underreport RMB rounds, showing only $20.7M vs. the ~$49M estimated by CheckThat.ai. This creates an information asymmetry: investors relying solely on Western databases will systematically underestimate Chinese AI infrastructure companies. The mechanism is regulatory (Chinese data is harder to capture) and the implication is that cross-border due diligence requires specialized data sources.

---"}
